item.title: Little piece of site history.
item.content:encoded: I just want to say I really like seeing the comments left on this article, watching people discuss the creation of an element of the foundation universe (outside individual skips) is really cool.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

The tale series Parable of the Wayward Prince can be called the closest thing the Alexylva University GoI has to a hub page, and serves a great place for anyone interested in reading about AU skips and tales. Linking this skip there would help facilitate such a leg of the wikiwalk. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

REMIT ORDER TO:
ALEXYLVA UNIVERSITY
PHYSICAL PLANT OPERATIONS
MOTORIZED TRANSPORTATION DEPARTMENT
CXI SECOND CIRCLE
ALEXANDRIA OF FORESTS

To this:

REMIT ORDER TO:
ALEXYLVA UNIVERSITY
PHYSICAL PLANT OPERATIONS
MOTORIZED TRANSPORTATION DEPARTMENT
CXI SECOND CIRCLE
ALEXANDRIA OF FORESTS

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one a lot, but there is one thing that's irking me. The article says specifically that no instructions were found with the object. That being said, how did the Foundation figure out that it works on kids? I'm unaware if there's an "If nothing else works, throw kids into it" protocol in Foundation research, which I would feel is very unlikely (if there is an established precident for that protocol, I apologize), but assuming there isn't, I would like an explaination of how it was discovered that this object works only on children.
I can't get enough of this University stuff. Great work.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is a CC BY-SA 2.0 image by Graham Horn via Wikipedia Commons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Strong upvote. I just love the implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it intended that this invoice is written in English rather than the usual Latin/Greek/Cherokee from Alexsylva stuff? I can't recall anything else of theirs that didn't have to be translated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess the term child feels emotionally evocative enough to make me eh, though I would prefer if the age range was in the first paragraph rather than the second, just for the sake of clarifying that child, for the purposes of this device, has a more specific definition than 'minor'.

Mea culpa on subjects, I could have sworn I saw other articles routinely using the term in the sense of 'test subjects we applied this SCP to'. I think a mention what's been done with previously created 1080-1 might be nice, but that's just a thought.

(I'm also really not trying to hunt down all your articles with intent of being an ass, BTW. >.> I really like the idea of Alexylva, so I've been reading up.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not really more clinical, though. It just uses more words. There's nothing specifically unclinical about "child"; it defines the parameters (juvenile human) and is followed by the clearest specific trait of that individual (no more than twelve). Also, you can't call it a "subject", since it isn't a subject. The subject of the article is the object that is SCP-1080. The children who are introduced into SCP-1080 aren't subjects. There might be a way to make it more clinical, but I'm not inclined to completely sacrifice narrative flow for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I realize this is old-ish, but I would like it a lot more if some of the language in the description was tweaked to be more clinical. For example, replacing "child' with "juvenile human aged no more than twelve (12) years" or some such in the first instance, and "subject" thereafter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Totally agreed. This creeps me out beyond recognition. +1

Edit: Wait, the quantity was X. Guess the university got the other nine w/o an invoice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. Color me intrigued.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sort of debating it. I'm definitely going to establish a pretty wide base before I build on it, though. Expect more low-level stuff before the rest of the iceberg comes out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that this and the other University objects are leading up to something bigger. Is that true, or is it more or less just a unifying theme for the articles?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, and the other University items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, that's pretty much exactly it. The fact that the last two were shipments was going to be coincidence, but now that you mention it, I might just run with that. I'm glad I'm getting the right message across.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The impression I get from this and the two other "University" articles is that the University exists in a parallel Earth where the manufacture and purchase of things we would consider SCPs is commonplace, and for some reason purchases that are shipped to the University have a tendency to shift into our universe before the University receives them. Is that the impression you were going for?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been thinking of it as Redacted University, actually. I was a bit worried that any name I gave it at this point would sound dumber than the blackboxes. When I come up with one, should I go back and edit the others to reflect it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eskobar, I like these, but please, give the University a name. A potential GoI with a blackboxed name is annoying to write, and harder to remember.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(clears throat, points at SCP-877 as well)

I may need to work on a Tale or something similar soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …With this and SCP-961, have we got another Group of Interest brewing?
